sentences.text: Right.
Yes, they did.
But I have no idea where they went.
Of course, we all--we all went down to southern France.
But once we got there, and I met my father, you know--
About two.
Oh, yeah.
About my age, my age.
And-- but there was nothing special I can--I remember, really.
No.
No, we-- that-- once I left Karlsruhe, I don't remember-- the last time I went to a syn-- oh, well-- well, even when I was bar mitzvahed in Marseille, that was not in a synagogue.
Yeah.
I can-- I recall that they--they had a synagogue there, too.
But we did not participate in that synagogue.
The [?
Werdheimers ?] were not as religious as we were, you know, in my hometown.
Well, I don't remember the exact--
--the exact date, but I think probably October, 22 1940.
Yeah, sure.
In other words, they didn't want us at all.
I mean, I was-- just a few days ago, I was climbing up on the-- towards the schloss in Heidelberg, the castle in Heidelberg.
And I came back.
And the next day, at dawn again-- the Germans are very good at that, at dawn-- and they had a-- they knocked at the door and told me we had 15 minutes to go.
We asked, where are we going?
Says, don't ask questions. 15 minutes.
And then we came down.
And there was already a mob of people waiting and throwing rocks at us.
But we-- we got into the truck.
And before you know, we were in a train station.
And there were thousands of Jews there, I mean, you know, waiting for the next step.
I didn't know at that time exactly where we were going to go.
But later on, I found out that while they were building these death camps, or those camps in-- in Eastern Europe, they would keep us over in southern France.
They found a place in southern France where they could hold us.
And so we went on the train.
And it was-- sure enough, before we knew, there were French names.
And we realized we were in France.
And-- and then we went down to southern France.
I don't remember, really.
I--I-- it's been such a long time ago.
I really couldn't tell you.
Well, the conditions was one of the old trains.
I mean, it was not one of these-- it was one of the old trains.
It was not one of those cattle cars.
But it was an old train.
And-- well, whatever, it may have taken a day to get down to southern France.
With everybody lived in the Heidelberg area.
And when we got there, there was a main street.
And as the trucks took us to our section, I-- I saw my cousin, [?
Monfried, ?] who also saw me.
And so he went right to get my dad.
And then my dad came out.
And-- and then I jumped out.
I jumped off the truck to-- to--to see my father.
And they just said, you can't do that.
And they pushed me-- they took me back into the truck.
But my father was able to arrange it-- I don't know how he did it-- arrange it so that I could be with him in that special-- in that ilot.
The sections they called, in that camp, was ilot, I-L-O-T, section-- the French name.
OK.
What was the name of the camp?
The Gurs.
G-U-R-S.
And-- well, it was-- it was-- there were thousands of people there.
And we were sectioned off in special sections.
In other words, I don't know how many people were in each section.
But there were thousands of them, and-- and there were barracks, all barracks.
And-- barracks.
There were no beds.
It was basically wooden floor.
And we had to sleep on a wooden floor.
And there was an oven, a stove in the middle to keep it warm.
And-- and there were heavy rains.
That was the rainy season or whatever, because every time you turn around, there was rain.
And when you got-- when went out, you were walking in the mud, deep mud like this.
And so it wasn't pleasant.
And people died there every day.
In other words, not--not-- they were not death camps yet.
But they were died of malnutrition, disease, hunger.
Because the food-- I mean, what was there-- I mean, you had three meals a day.
You had a piece of bread in the morning, French bread, piece of French bread and some coffee.
And then in the-- noon time, you had another coffee.
And then at night, you had soup, and--and-- and that's it.
A piece of bread in the morning would have to last the rest of the day.
No.
Because there was-- there was a number of Spanish people, who were fighting in that civil war and who were able to get out.
When Franco took over, they had to get out, unless they want to get killed.
And-- and the French let them in.
And this was one of the camps that there were thousands of them, too, thousands of them that-- Spanish Republicans, they called themselves Republicans.
And they were there.
And the rest of them were either French or German.
There were also some French cit-- Jews there, too.
No, that's it.
There were thousands of them.
And the-- the toilet facility was very interesting.
I mean, each ilot had a-- just holes, a bunch of holes.
And you had to sort of fend for yourself.
And they had some wooden boards or whatever, you know, but that was it.
You really-- I think there was only one shower in the whole camp.
And we, once in a while, would end up over there to--to take a shower.
About once a week.
Yes.
He was also in the same-- in the same barrack, in the same place.
He was about two years older than I was.
And--yeah.
About six months.
Six months.
Well, there wasn't anything you could do except-- except be idle, loiter around.
There was nothing--nothing for you to do.
And it was just-- and you know, when you're--when you're idle and you don't do anything-- you don't have anything to do, you know, you can go-- you can go crazy.
By they way, this camp was controlled by the Vichy guards.
In other words, the Vichy guards, the French government had collaborated with the Germans.
Their-- it was their guards, but run by German officers, definitely German officers.
And-- And there was--there was once, I think, once that we tried.
We just walked out the camp, you know, just give you an example.
And you know, we walked out and went into the French village, where we able-- were able to get food, you know.
And remember, my mother sent some money, you know, so I had some money.
But--
Oh, yes.
She kept in touch with us all the time, every way possible.
They didn't take it too seriously.
They didn't take-- I mean-- I mean, if they would have taken the job seriously, they wouldn't have let me go out.
You see?
But when I came back, German officer saw me.
So he--he pushed me into his-- into the office, in his headquarters, and says, you don't do that again.
And if you do it, you're gonna get some-- something's going to happen to you.
And he sort of kicked me out.
But a few days later, I went again.
I took a chance.
I mean-- I mean, there was not enough food.
And where'd I get the food?
In the village.
And then I brought back some to the barrack, to the people who were in that particular barrack.
We didn't-- we didn't have enough food for everybody.
What was the talk in the camp, in the Gurs, at that time?
Nobody realized at that time that Hitler was going to kill the Jews, kill them all.
There was not-- no one, at that point-- that was in 1941-- realized that he would do this, that he would build these gas chambers and all that.
You know, that was not yet in our mind at all.

We just thought we were typical prisoners of war, or prisoners, whatever, and that we would probably have to stay there for-- for a long time, or whatever, a short time, until-- until the war is over.
But it didn't happen.
Oh, yeah.
I'm pretty sure he-- he had second thoughts, you know.
But it was too late.
The-- the damage was done.
He made the decision.
But in the same hand, he also made it possible for us, for him and me, to eventually get out of that mess.
No.
Nobody knew at that time.
No one knew.
Because remember, the Germans didn't make a decision until January of 1942.
Decision was, how to solve the Jewish-- how to, how to-- the Jewish problem.
And--and the decision was, at that time, that they would build these camps, these gas chambers and all that, in-- in Eastern Europe.
And then they would be able to solve the problem of the-- of the Jews.
Nobody knew.
I mean, you saw-- I--I-- I showed you a picture of where we saw a soccer game in Les Milles.
Nobody, at that time, knew what was going to-- that was the second camp.
Well, it was there for six months.
And then-- then we went to Les Milles.
Well, my-- my father was able to manipulate, bribe the guards or the officials, to--to write out something where it says liberation, you know, you're liberated.
Make it official, because you have to have something, you know.
And then his job-- I was free.
But he-- he would stay there for a while longer.
And then he had to do the same thing for himself.
And keep in mind that after-- after I was free--
Well, I went to a Quaker home nearby, which-- which I was hiding for about three months.
was a castle.
It was one of those little castles.
And it had all the Spanish children, the ones that escaped from-- from Spain, from the Spanish Civil War.
They were there.
They were only two German Jews there, just two German Jews.
And-- and we were hiding.
In other words, basically, this is how you hide when you-- you know, it's a Spanish speaking area, you know.
Well, we had to work, and-- there was a farm nearby.
We had to work on the farm to keep ourselves busy.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
Oh, you know, I was a stamp collector, you know.
So one of them got hold of my stamps.
I don't know how I even got the stamps in there, but-- they were not exactly nice people.
You know, in other words, my-- they weren't exactly nice.
But they didn't-- they didn't report us, anyway.
They didn't report us to the authorities.
I'm pretty sure they knew who we were.
But--
Well, I guess we-- my father made all the arrangements.
In other words, he saw there was a--a-- a--a--a Quaker home nearby.
And he said, let me put you over there.
And there were no difficulties at all.
I just walked in.
And they took me.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] about 300.
I'm pretty sure they knew.
They knew.
But they were concerned about other problems.
They weren't concerned about me, except that they stole my stamp collection.
[CHUCKLES]
Always.
It was always there, always there.
I was-- look, as far as I'm concerned, I was always afraid.
I mean, you know, I mean, I'm not a hero.
In other words, I was afraid.

And-- and I think when the German government, the German officials, decided to come over and--and inspect the Quaker home, the Quakers took me away and put it-- put me into a-- a convent or nunnery, whatever it is, Catholic nunnery.
And they kept me for two weeks.
Yes.
Mm-hmm.
I don't know what happened to him.
I really don't know.
I don't know what happened to him.
But--
About two weeks.
Can you describe that?
Do you have any recollections?
Well, the only recollection I have is that--that we had to go to services, you know, their services.
And I was supposed to have kneeled and I didn't kneel.
They didn't like it.
But what are you going to do?
And they said, all right, he's a Jewish boy.
So you don't die.
I guess you don't do it.
In other words, leave him alone.
They-- they were very helpful, really.
They were thoughtful.
I really thought a lot about these Catholic nuns that really were great.
I mean, they're talking about-- today, you know, that there's a lot of anti-Semitism in--in France.
Of course, there probably is.
But the point is that my recollection is I had some-- I really-- I was really wel-protected by these Catholic nuns.
Yes.
We weren't interested in that at all.
We were-- played ball.
Little Spanish kids.
Played basketball and-- and soccer.
And soccer.
That the European [INAUDIBLE] football.
After the-- oh, after the convent.
Well, I came back to the-- to the Spanish-- to the Quaker home.
But then I found out my father was able to get some kind of certificate, too.
So he and I met in Marseille.
And then in Marseille, I was-- became bar mitzvahed.
Bar mitzvahed in Marseille, in one of the Jewish homes.
Again, my father was able to find some Jewish people in Marseille.
And you know, I had a pretty good background in Hebrew and all that, so-- and Jewish prayer, so it was no problem.
Yeah, that's right.
That's right.
In other words-- of course, I didn't do too much, either.
You know, I mean, I think I just said the major baruch, you know, the-- before and after the reading of the Torah.
Not much, no.
No, no.
This was just a-- a spirit of the moment type of thing.
But then what we decided to do was to go to Spain, and through-- go through Spain to Portugal, where there was an ocean liner.
But when we got to the Spanish border, they refused.
We had a great deal of hope.
We thought--
Hope to be able to get away from here, to get out, out of the danger.
Of course, we knew eventually, whoever-- the Jews that were left in France were also going to be deported over to Eastern Europe.
It was just a matter of time.
So-- so we were--
Oh, yeah, because Eastern Europe was like the-- there's no stop after that.
Eastern Europe was more or less the-- the place where most Jews would end up.
And-- so nobody wanted to go to Eastern Europe.
I mean, we've already heard some of the bad things about Eastern Europe.
They didn't have those death camps yet.
But they already knew there were some very bad things going on over there.
Yeah, right.
Well, we-- at least we got out.
I mean, we eventually got out.
But there were a lot of people, a lot of these people who stayed behind, who eventually were deported.
The Jewish people in France, in southern France especially.
Well, a ship was still there, in Lisbon.
So we tried-- we tried another approach.
We tried the-- using a merchant ship, a merchant-- French merchant ship that would take us to Casablanca.
That's where-- that's where the--the ship would stop, at Casablanca.
So the merchant ship was a small ship, you know, not an ocean liner.
It was just a merchant ship.
And we stopped over at Algiers-- Algiers, Tangiers, Tangiers, Tangier.
Well, Les Milles.
I forgot about Les Milles.
Well, that happened after Gurs.
In other words, we went right to Les Milles.
And Les Milles had a lot of Jews that had documents that-- documents that would say that, yes, you can come into the United States.
In other words, a lot of-- a lot of Jews who had intention of going to the United States, for example, were in Les Milles, more than-- than anyone else.
No.
They were all Jews.
Les Milles was near Marseille, near Marseille, France.
Well, the point is, my father was able to convince the authorities that we would be able to leave France.
And-- and when they heard that, they said, OK, we'll give you a pass to go to Les Milles.
And then from there, it wouldn't be too difficult to-- to move on to--
Yeah, it was one big factory building, enormous, enormous building.
It was not like--like Gurs.
Gurs had a lot of barracks, nothing but barracks.
But the factory building, it means everybody had his little space.
And as far as I know, they even had beds, I mean, wooden beds, you know, where-- port-- you know, crafted-- you know--you know, sort of-- I can't explain it to you.
But it's not the way we pick up a furniture piece today.
It was rough edges.
Well, the living condition of Les Milles was better.
Oh, yeah, it was better because you didn't have-- didn't have heavy rain.
And even it rained, it wouldn't do much damage because it was concrete, most of it.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, sure.
All I remember is that-- the Vichy French, Vichy French government, basically.
Again, it may be also-- there maybe German command.
But I--I didn't see the German command.
Yeah.
Manfred.
We--we did not hear anything about it until-- the last time we heard about Manfred is when we were in the United States, and we heard that he did make it.
I never asked him how he made it.
But he somehow-- all I know is that he said that the und-- the French underground helped him.
Three months.
That was 1941, 1941.
Yeah, I would say April, May, June, 1940.
'41, excuse me. 1941.
Then I went to the-- to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the French home, the Quaker home.
I had to stay there.
I stayed there until my father was able to get out.
Well, in the fall-- in the fall of 1940, we-- we-- we got together.
And then we planned our trip first of all to go-- to try to go to Spain.
And then secondly, we-- we tried to take a boat, a ship to Casablanca.
There were two ways of going.
Yeah.
Of course, yeah.
You had-- you had to buy them.
No, we didn't go to Portugal.
We never made it.
In other words, what happened in Portugal, the ship-- the ocean liner was waiting in-- in-- in Lisbon, but we were going to meet that ship in Casablanca.
And we did, by the way.
Eventually we did.
Right.
